title: The Picture of Dorian Gray
description: The Picture of Dorian Gray — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A comprehensive encyclopedia of characters, themes, and events in Oscar Wilde's The Picture of Dorian Gray

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: "Nowadays a broken heart will run to many editions"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton cynically observes that poets exploit their suffering for publication, suggesting that emotional pain is a marketable commodity."

memes.label: "That a burnt child loves the fire"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton inverts the proverb to assert the seductive power of danger and forbidden experience."

relations.label: Courage has passed from men to women

memes.label: A Grande Passion Is the Privilege of People Who Have Nothing to Do
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram dismissing grand romantic passion as a luxury of the idle classes."

memes.label: A Man Can Be Happy with Any Woman
memes.description: Lord Henry's cynical aphorism on marriage — that happiness with a woman requires not loving her.
aliases: as long as he does not love her

memes.label: "His beauty had been to him but a mask, his youth but a mockery"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray, in a moment of violent self-reckoning, smashes the mirror Lord Henry gave him and denounces his own beauty and youth as instruments of his ruin."

relations.label: It had been like conscience to him

memes.label: "A mist makes things wonderful"
memes.description: "Lord Henry tells the Duchess of Monmouth that certainty is fatal and that uncertainty — like a mist — is what makes life beautiful and mysterious."

memes.label: A New Hedonism
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's call for a modern form of hedonism — a philosophy of pleasure and sensation that would liberate the individual from Victorian moral constraints and become the guiding creed of Dorian Gray's life. (002-048)

memes.label: "a new Hedonism that was to recreate life"
memes.description: "The narrator describes the new Hedonism that Dorian Gray embraces — a philosophy of experience that seeks to recreate life through the senses and intellect."

memes.label: "A new life!"
memes.description: "In the final chapter, Dorian Gray resolves to begin a new life — to be good and to spare innocence — in a moment of apparent moral awakening that the portrait will soon expose as vanity."

relations.label: She knew nothing, but she had everything that he had lost
It had been like conscience to him
It would kill the past, and when that was dead, he would be free

memes.label: Acting
memes.description: The art of theatrical performance that Wilde uses to explore the boundary between authentic emotion and aesthetic display, most centrally through Sibyl Vane's acting career.

memes.label: Actual Life Was Chaos
memes.description: The narrative meditation on Dorian's terror — "Actual life was chaos, but there was something terribly logical in the imagination" — revealing the psychological mechanism by which conscience manufactures punishment.
aliases: imagination set remorse to dog the feet of sin

memes.label: Adrian Singleton
memes.description: A young aristocrat and former companion of Dorian Gray whose ruin through debauchery serves as a visible warning of the destructive path Dorian himself walks. (012-019)

memes.label: Alan Campbell
memes.description: A brilliant young chemist and former intimate friend of Dorian Gray, whom Dorian blackmails into disposing of Basil Hallward's body — an act that ultimately leads to Campbell's suicide.

memes.label: Alan Campbell's Disposal of Basil's Body
memes.description: Dorian Gray blackmails chemist Alan Campbell into disposing of Basil Hallward's body using chemicals, exploiting a shared secret from their past.

memes.label: All Art Is Quite Useless
memes.description: "The final sentence of Wilde's Preface: art's value lies precisely in its freedom from utility."

memes.label: All crime is vulgar
memes.description: "All crime is vulgar, just as all vulgarity is crime."

relations.label: only horrible thing in the world is ennui
The books that the world calls immoral are books that show the world its own shame

memes.label: All Influence Is Immoral
memes.description: "Lord Henry's anti-credo: there is no such thing as a good influence — all influence is immoral from the scientific point of view."
aliases: no such thing as good influence

relations.label: the only way to get rid of a temptation is to yield to it

memes.label: "All ways end at the same point — Disillusion"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton tells the Duchess of Monmouth that all paths of inquiry and experience converge in disillusionment."

relations.label: only horrible thing in the world is ennui

memes.label: "I have known everything, but I am always ready for a new emotion"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton declares his world-weariness and his hunger for novelty, capturing the paradox of the dandy: jaded yet insatiable."

memes.label: "There is always something ridiculous about the emotions"
memes.description: "The narrator's devastating observation that emotions become absurd when love has ended — a sentence that marks Dorian's first act of emotional cruelty toward Sibyl Vane."

relations.label: It is only shallow people who require years to get rid of an emotion

memes.label: Always! That Is a Dreadful Word
memes.description: Lord Henry's dismissal of permanence — "the only difference between a caprice and a lifelong passion is that the caprice lasts a little longer" — articulating the novel's ethic of impermanence and the rejection of enduring commitment.

memes.label: American Girls Are as Clever at Concealing Their Parents
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram comparing American girls concealing parents to English women concealing their past."

memes.label: "An Artist Should Put Nothing of His Own Life"
memes.description: Basil Hallward's declaration of artistic principle — "An artist should create beautiful things, but should put nothing of his own life into them" — the creed he has betrayed by painting Dorian Gray's portrait, spoken in Chapter 1.

memes.label: Anybody Can Be Good in the Country
memes.description: "Lord Henry's rejoinder when Dorian claims to have done a good deed: 'Anybody can be good in the country. There are no temptations there.'"

memes.label: "Anything becomes a pleasure if one does it too often"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton articulates the decadent psychology of habituation: repetition transforms even transgression into pleasure."

memes.label: Appearance and Reality
memes.description: The central thematic opposition in the novel, embodied in the relationship between Dorian Gray's unchanging outward appearance and the hideously transformed portrait that reveals his true moral condition.

memes.label: The British Aristocracy
memes.description: The hereditary ruling class of Victorian Britain, to which Dorian Gray belongs by birth, and whose values, privileges, and moral hypocrisy the novel anatomizes.

memes.label: Art and Life
memes.description: The complex, bidirectional relationship between artistic representation and lived experience — the novel's philosophical core, literalized in the portrait that blurs the boundary between art and reality.

memes.label: Art Has No Influence Upon Action
memes.description: "Art has no influence upon action. It annihilates the desire to act. It is superbly sterile. The books that the world calls immoral are books that show the world its own shame."

memes.label: Artists and Models
memes.description: The artist-model relationship in The Picture of Dorian Gray, centered on Basil Hallward's worship of Dorian Gray as both muse and subject, and the power dynamics that flow from this dynamic.

memes.label: Basil Confronts Dorian
memes.description: Basil Hallward confronts Dorian Gray about the terrible rumours surrounding his life, sees the hideously transformed portrait, and urges him to repent—moments before Dorian murders him.

memes.label: Basil Hallward
memes.description: The artist who paints Dorian Gray's portrait — a man of genuine moral seriousness whose devotion to beauty and to Dorian blinds him to the danger he has helped create.

memes.label: Basil Hallward's Studio
memes.description: The artist's workspace where the portrait of Dorian Gray is painted and the fateful wish is made — the novel's foundational setting.

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Central aesthetic concept in The Picture of Dorian Gray — physical perfection that inspires art, becomes an object of worship, and ultimately reveals the corruption beneath the surface.

memes.label: Beauty and Morality
memes.description: "The central aesthetic and ethical tension in the novel: the question of whether beauty and art exist in a realm separate from conventional morality, or whether the pursuit of beauty inevitably entails moral consequences."

memes.label: Beauty Ends Where Intellect Begins
memes.description: Lord Henry's pronouncement on the incompatibility of beauty and thought — that intellect deforms the harmony of the face — in his first long speech defending Dorian's appearance.
aliases: real beauty ends where an intellectual expression begins

memes.label: Beauty Is a Form of Genius
memes.description: Lord Henry's declaration that beauty surpasses intellect — the core of his aesthetic philosophy and the novel's meditation on the power of physical appearance.

memes.label: Because You Have the Most Marvellous Youth
memes.description: Lord Henry's reply when Dorian asks why his influence cannot be bad — "Because you have the most marvellous youth, and youth is the one thing worth having" — the opening salvo of the novel's most famous speech on youth and beauty.
aliases: youth is the one thing worth having

memes.label: Behind Every Exquisite Thing
memes.description: The narrator's meditation on the hidden tragedy underlying all beauty — a thematic key to the novel's treatment of Dorian Gray's origins and fate.
aliases: something tragic behind every exquisite thing

memes.label: Being Adored Is a Nuisance
memes.description: Lord Henry compares being adored to the burden of divinity — "Being adored is a nuisance. Women treat us just as humanity treats its gods. They worship us, and are always bothering us to do something for them."

memes.label: Being Natural Is Simply a Pose
memes.description: "Lord Henry's paradox: sincerity itself is a performance, and naturalness the most studied of all poses."

memes.label: "What was that loathsome red dew that gleamed... as though the canvas had sweated blood?"
memes.description: "The narrative voice describes Dorian Gray's horrified discovery of fresh blood on the portrait's hand after Basil Hallward's murder — the portrait's most visceral manifestation of conscience."

relations.label: It had been like conscience to him

memes.label: Brute Reason Is Unbearable
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram on rationality: I can stand brute force, but brute reason is quite unbearable — it is hitting below the intellect."

memes.label: "Why is it that I cannot feel this tragedy as much as I want to?"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray confesses to Lord Henry his inability to feel the full weight of Sibyl Vane's suicide, a moment that marks the beginning of his moral hardening."

memes.label: Caprice and Passion
memes.description: "Lord Henry's aphorism on the temporality of passion: the only difference between a caprice and a lifelong passion is that the caprice lasts a little longer."
aliases: difference between caprice and lifelong passion

memes.label: Children Begin by Loving Their Parents
memes.description: "A narrative observation on the evolution of filial love: children begin by loving their parents, then judge them, and sometimes forgive them — from the narrator's perspective on James Vane."

memes.label: "Choice Is Taken from Them"
memes.description: The narrator's reflection on the psychology of sin — how passion for sin strips away free will, leaving conscience "either killed" or turned into a servant of rebellion.

memes.label: "A Cigarette Is the Perfect Pleasure"
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's aestheticist manifesto in miniature — "A cigarette is the perfect type of a perfect pleasure. It is exquisite, and it leaves one unsatisfied. What more can one want?" — spoken at lunch with Dorian Gray and Basil Hallward in Chapter 6.
aliases: perfect type of a perfect pleasure

memes.label: Civilization
memes.description: A concept that in The Picture of Dorian Gray stands for the veneer of respectability that masks the primitive impulses beneath London high society (011-016).

memes.label: Concealment
memes.description: A central structural principle in The Picture of Dorian Gray governing the relationship between art and artist, public face and private sin, and the portrait's function as the concealed record of Dorian's soul.

memes.label: Confess Your Sin
memes.description: James Vane's demand that Dorian Gray face judgment — "You had better confess your sin, for as sure as I am James Vane, you are going to die" — the climax of the revenge plot.
aliases: you had better confess your sin

memes.label: Confession
memes.description: The act of revealing hidden truths — about oneself, about art, about sin — that recurs as both a moral impulse and a dangerous exposure throughout the novel.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The moral faculty that Dorian Gray progressively suppresses — and that ultimately returns to destroy him — representing the inescapable claim of ethical judgment upon even the most determined hedonist.

memes.label: Conscience and Cowardice
memes.description: Lord Henry's aphorism equating conscience with cowardice — a foundational statement of the novel's amoral philosophy.
aliases: conscience is the trade-name of the firm

memes.label: Conscience Could Raise Such Fearful Phantoms
memes.description: Dorian Gray's terrified reflection that "conscience could raise such fearful phantoms, and give them visible form" — the novel's most acute description of guilt's psychological reality.

memes.label: Conscience is the divinest thing in us
memes.description: "I know what conscience is, to begin with. It is not what you told me it was. It is the divinest thing in us."

memes.label: Conscience makes egotists of us all
memes.description: Lord Henry dismisses conscience as a form of self-regard — being told one is a sinner merely inflates one's sense of importance — in his extended consolation to Dorian after Sibyl Vane's suicide.

memes.label: Corruption
memes.description: The progressive moral and spiritual decay of Dorian Gray, externalized through the transformation of his portrait and documented across the novel from his first hidden sin to his final destruction (010-011).

memes.label: "You have gone from corruption to corruption, and now you have culminated in crime"
memes.description: "Alan Campbell delivers the novel's most direct moral condemnation of Dorian Gray — tracing his trajectory from corruption to crime in a single damning sentence."

relations.label: It had been like conscience to him

memes.label: "Courage has passed from men to women"
memes.description: "The Duchess of Monmouth turns the tables on Lord Henry, declaring a historical shift in gendered virtue."

relations.label: That a burnt child loves the fire

memes.label: Covent Garden
memes.description: The great London flower and vegetable market where Dorian Gray wanders at dawn after cruelly rejecting Sibyl Vane — a scene of floral beauty that offers transient consolation and marks the first full expression of the novel's aesthetic refuge from moral consequence.

memes.label: Credit Is the Capital of a Younger Son
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton's epigram on the economics of aristocratic younger sons: credit substitutes for inheritance."

memes.label: Crime
memes.description: The terminal stage of Dorian Gray's moral decay, culminating in the murder of Basil Hallward and the blackmail-driven suicide of Alan Campbell (014-081).

memes.label: "Crime belongs exclusively to the lower orders"
memes.description: "Lord Henry reassures Dorian that he is incapable of murder, explaining that crime — like art — is merely a method of procuring extraordinary sensations, and belongs only to the lower classes."

relations.label: All crime is vulgar, just as all vulgarity is crime.
Form is absolutely essential to it.
Each of us has Heaven and Hell in him.

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: A recurring motif in The Picture of Dorian Gray, manifesting as the physical mark of sin on Dorian's portrait and the psychological cruelty inherent in pleasure-seeking without conscience.

memes.label: "in nearly every joy, cruelty has its place"
memes.description: "The narrator observes that Dorian Gray took cruel pleasure in reading about his fictional counterpart's decay, introducing the novel's dark insight that cruelty and joy are intertwined."

memes.label: "One is by being cultured, the other by being corrupt"
memes.description: "Lord Henry tells Dorian that civilization can only be reached through two paths — culture or corruption — dismissing country life as stagnation."

memes.label: Cure the Soul by Means of the Senses
memes.description: Lord Henry's formulation of his hedonistic philosophy — that the senses and soul are reciprocal cures — which becomes Dorian Gray's guiding principle.

memes.label: Curiosity
memes.description: A driving psychological force in The Picture of Dorian Gray, manifesting as Lord Henry's intellectual voyeurism and Dorian's insatiable desire to explore all experiences life offers (004-038).

memes.label: Curzon Street
memes.description: The Mayfair street where Lord Henry Wotton lives — the setting of his invitation to Dorian Gray in Chapter 2 and the starting point of his stroll to the Albany in Chapter 3, anchoring Lord Henry's identity in the fashionable West End.

memes.label: "Death and vulgarity are the only two facts"
memes.description: "Lord Henry declares that death and vulgarity are the only things that cannot be explained away — a characteristically flippant remark with dark resonance in a novel haunted by murder."

memes.label: Death and Vulgarity Are the Only Two Facts
memes.description: "Lord Henry: 'Death and vulgarity are the only two facts in the nineteenth century that one cannot explain away' — a characteristic dismissal delivered as Dorian has just confessed to murder."

memes.label: Death of James Vane
memes.description: James Vane, pursuing vengeance for his sister Sibyl, is accidentally shot dead by Sir Geoffrey Clouston during a hunting party at Selby Royal.

memes.label: Decadence
memes.description: The late nineteenth-century artistic and literary movement characterized by a rejection of conventional morality, a fascination with artifice and sensation, and an embrace of the artificial over the natural — themes central to the world of Dorian Gray.

memes.label: "Decay fascinates me more"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton inverts the Victorian creed of progress, declaring decay more aesthetically compelling than development."

relations.label: It represents the survival of the pushing

memes.label: Deception
memes.description: The practice of concealment and misrepresentation that pervades every relationship in The Picture of Dorian Gray, from social politeness to murderous cover-up (001-017).

memes.label: Desire
memes.description: The force of wanting — for beauty, for experience, for forbidden things — that drives the major characters and shapes the novel's moral landscape.

memes.label: Destiny Does Not Send Us Heralds
memes.description: "As for omens, there is no such thing as an omen. Destiny does not send us heralds. She is too wise or too cruel for that."
aliases: destiny does not send us heralds
no such thing as an omen

relations.label: only horrible thing in the world is ennui

memes.label: "Destiny Never Closed Her Accounts"
memes.description: A narrator's reflection on fate's inexorable accounting — the philosophical frame for James Vane's return and the novel's revenge plot in Chapter 16.

memes.label: Dorian Gray
memes.description: The protagonist — a young man of extraordinary beauty who makes a Faustian bargain to preserve his youth while his portrait bears the marks of age and sin.

memes.label: Dorian Gray's Transformation
memes.description: The moral and psychological degeneration of Dorian Gray over eighteen years, enabled by the supernatural portrait that bears the visible consequences of his sins while his outward appearance remains untouched.

memes.label: Dorian Hides the Portrait
memes.description: Dorian Gray moves the changing portrait to the old schoolroom, covering it with a purple-and-gold pall and locking it away — his first active concealment of his moral decay.

memes.label: Dorian Makes His Wish
memes.description: The pivotal scene in Basil Hallward's studio where Dorian Gray, upon seeing his finished portrait, wishes that it would age in his place — setting the novel's supernatural mechanism in motion.

memes.label: Dorian's Attempted Reform
memes.description: Haunted by guilt, Dorian Gray spares a village girl from seduction, only to find his "good deed" has deepened the portrait's corruption with hypocrisy rather than reversed it.

memes.label: Death of Dorian Gray
memes.description: Dorian Gray stabs his portrait to destroy the evidence of his sins, only to die himself — his body becoming withered and loathsome while the painting returns to its original beauty.

memes.label: Dorian's Engagement to Sibyl
memes.description: Dorian Gray meets the young actress Sibyl Vane at a shabby theatre, falls passionately in love, and announces his engagement—a romance built on art and illusion that will end in tragedy.

memes.label: Double Life
memes.description: The central predicament of Dorian Gray, who maintains a facade of youthful innocence in society while leading a secret life of corruption and vice, enabled by the supernatural portrait that bears the marks of his sins. (015-001)

memes.label: Dreadful Things Are Being Said
memes.description: Basil Hallward's grave warning to Dorian Gray — "the most dreadful things are being said against you in London" — the opening move in the Chapter 12 confrontation that leads to Basil's death.
aliases: the most dreadful things are being said against you

relations.label: I love scandals about other people

memes.label: Duality
memes.description: The division between public appearance and private reality — the central psychological condition of Dorian Gray, whose preserved beauty conceals a corrupted soul.

memes.label: Duchess of Harley
memes.description: A good-natured society hostess who attends Lady Agatha's dinner party in Chapter 3 and participates in the conversation about American manners. (003-036)

memes.label: Duchess of Monmouth
memes.description: A beautiful young society hostess, married to an elderly duke, who fascinates

memes.label: "Each man lived his own life and paid his own price for living it"
memes.description: "After encountering the ruined Adrian Singleton, Dorian Gray rationalises his refusal of moral responsibility with an aphoristic declaration of radical individualism — each man pays his own price."

relations.label: You have gone from corruption to corruption, and now you have culminated in crime

memes.label: Each of Us Has Heaven and Hell
memes.description: Dorian Gray's anguished cry to Basil Hallward — "Each of us has heaven and hell in him" — a moment of self-revelation that nearly exposes his secret life.

memes.label: Each of Us Has Heaven and Hell in Him
memes.description: "Dorian to Basil: Each of us has heaven and hell in him, Basil — a wild gesture of despair before the hideous portrait."
aliases: each of us has heaven and hell

memes.label: Each Time That One Loves Is the Only Time
memes.description: "Each time that one loves is the only time one has ever loved. Difference of object does not alter singleness of passion."

memes.label: East End
memes.description: The impoverished district of Victorian London where Dorian Gray engages in philanthropic work, serving as a symbolic counterpoint to the wealth of Mayfair (001-071).

memes.label: "Eighteen Years! Set Me Under the Lamp"
memes.description: Dorian Gray's triumphant challenge to James Vane — his youthful face disproves eighteen years of age and saves his life in the opium den.

memes.label: Enamoured of His Own Beauty
memes.description: "The narrator describes Dorian's secret ritual: 'He grew more and more enamoured of his own beauty, more and more interested in the corruption of his own soul.'"

memes.label: Ennui
memes.description: The profound spiritual weariness and world-weariness that haunts the characters of the novel — a signature malady of the decadent sensibility that Lord Henry calls "the one sin for which there is no forgiveness." (018-025)

memes.label: Epigram
memes.description: The rhetorical device of the epigram — a concise, witty, and often paradoxical statement — is the signature mode of Lord Henry Wotton's speech and a defining stylistic feature of the novel.

memes.label: Euston Road
memes.description: The dreary north London road where the Vane family lives in shabby gentility — the novel's emblem of the impoverished respectability that Sibyl Vane's theatrical career barely sustains, and the world James Vane leaves behind for Australia.

memes.label: Every Effect That One Produces Gives One an Enemy
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram: every achievement creates an enemy, and popularity requires mediocrity — a theory of reputation as social warfare."
aliases: good reputations are like good hats

memes.label: "Every Face That He Drew Seemed to Have a Fantastic Likeness to Basil Hallward"
memes.description: The morning after the murder, Dorian's idle sketches betray him — every face he draws unconsciously resembles the man he has just killed.

memes.label: Every Impulse We Strive to Strangle Broods in the Mind
memes.description: Lord Henry's argument for yielding to temptation — the psychological doctrine that repression poisons the soul and that action is a form of purification — the ethical core of the philosophy that will govern Dorian Gray's life.
aliases: the only way to get rid of a temptation is to yield to it

memes.label: Every Portrait Is a Portrait of the Artist
memes.description: "Basil Hallward's fundamental aesthetic creed: 'Every portrait that is painted with feeling is a portrait of the artist, not of the sitter.'"

memes.label: Evil as a Mode Through Which to Realize Beauty
memes.description: "There were moments when he looked on evil simply as a mode through which he could realize his conception of the beautiful."
aliases: realize his conception of the beautiful

memes.label: Examinations Are Pure Humbug
memes.description: Lord Fermor's dismissal of examinations as a test of nothing worth knowing — the gentleman already knows enough, and the non-gentleman can know nothing worth having — a pure expression of aristocratic anti-intellectualism.
aliases: examinations sir are pure humbug from beginning to end

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: In Wilde's novel, experience is both the raw material of life and its highest value — Lord Henry declares that "every experience is of value," yet the novel ultimately questions whether the relentless pursuit of experience leads to fulfillment or exhaustion. (006-019)

memes.label: Faithfulness Is a Confession of Failure
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram on fidelity: Faithfulness is to the emotional life what consistency is to the life of the intellect — simply a confession of failure."

memes.label: It Is the Faithless Who Know Love's Tragedies
memes.description: "Lord Henry's paradox of love: the faithful experience only its trivial surface; the faithless alone know its true depths and tragedies."
aliases: faithful know trivial side of love

memes.label: A Fatality About All Distinction
memes.description: Basil Hallward's sombre warning that all forms of distinction — beauty, art, wealth, intellect — are visited by a tragic fatality, spoken in reply to Lord Henry's dismissal of his fears about exhibiting the portrait.
aliases: a fatality about all physical and intellectual distinction

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: A pervasive emotion in The Picture of Dorian Gray, fear drives characters to conceal their true selves, avoid moral reckoning, and ultimately confront the terror of what they have become.

memes.label: Forgiveness Impossible, Forgetfulness Possible
memes.description: Dorian Gray, en route to the opium den, confronts the irreversibility of murder — "though forgiveness was impossible, forgetfulness was possible still."

memes.label: "Form is absolutely essential to it"
memes.description: "The narrator declares that form is the essential principle of both art and good society, collapsing the boundary between aesthetic and social judgment."

relations.label: All crime is vulgar, just as all vulgarity is crime.
Crime belongs exclusively to the lower orders.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: An ambiguous ideal in Wilde's novel — both the goal of self-realization and an illusion that conceals deeper forms of bondage. (018-009)

memes.label: Friendship
memes.description: The social bond that structures the central relationships of the novel — friendship in The Picture of Dorian Gray is shown to be a force capable of both creative inspiration and fatal corruption, shaped by influence, aesthetic worship, and self-interest.

memes.label: From a Label There Is No Escape
memes.description: "When Dorian calls Lord Henry 'Prince Paradox,' Lord Henry protests: 'From a label there is no escape! I refuse the title.'"

memes.label: "From a label there is no escape!"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton protests being labelled 'Prince Paradox' — a witty refusal that simultaneously confirms the label's accuracy."

memes.label: Genius Lasts Longer Than Beauty
memes.description: "Lord Henry's consolation to Basil: genius endures while beauty fades, which explains why we fill our minds with 'rubbish and facts' in the struggle for survival."

memes.label: Geoffrey Clouston
memes.description: A country gentleman and guest at Lord Henry's shooting party whose accidental shooting of James Vane resolves the novel's revenge plot. (015-064)

memes.label: Good Resolutions Are Always Made Too Late
memes.description: Dorian Gray, echoing Lord Henry's doctrine, reflects that good resolutions are inevitably made too late to be of any use — spoken the morning after Sibyl Vane's suicide.

memes.label: Good Resolutions Are Simply Cheques
memes.description: "Good resolutions are simply cheques that men draw on a bank where they have no account."

memes.label: Good Resolutions Are Useless Attempts
memes.description: Lord Henry's dismissal of moral resolve as vanity — good resolutions are "cheques that men draw on a bank where they have no account."
aliases: cheques drawn on a bank with no account

memes.label: Grosvenor Square
memes.description: Dorian Gray's Mayfair residence on Grosvenor Square — the fashionable West End address that anchors his social identity and serves as the novel's principal offstage domestic space, where Alan Campbell visits and Basil Hallward is last seen alive.

memes.label: Grown Sick of Shadows
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's declaration of liberation from the illusory world of theatre through love — "I have grown sick of shadows. You are more to me than all art can ever be."
aliases: more to me than all art

memes.label: Grown to Love Secrecy
memes.description: "Basil Hallward's confession: I have grown to love secrecy — it seems to be the one thing that can make modern life mysterious or marvellous to us."

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: The psychological burden of wrongdoing that haunts characters throughout the novel — from Dorian's tortured conscience to the guilt that Lord Henry's epigrams attempt to dismiss. (003-071)

memes.label: Happy With Any Woman
memes.description: "Lord Henry's dinner-party epigram: 'A man can be happy with any woman, as long as he does not love her.'"
aliases: a man can be happy with any woman

relations.label: A man can be happy with any woman

memes.label: The Harmony of Soul and Body
memes.description: "Basil Hallward's lament that modernity has separated soul from body: 'We in our madness have separated the two, and have invented a realism that is vulgar, an ideality that is void.'"

memes.label: Harry Spends His Days in Saying
memes.description: Dorian affectionately characterises Lord Henry's way of life — "Harry spends his days in saying what is incredible and his evenings in doing what is improbable."

memes.label: "Harry spends his days in saying what is incredible"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray affectionately characterizes Lord Henry's conversational style — epigrams that invert expectation — while revealing the influence Harry has over him."

memes.label: "He Has Sold Himself to the Devil for a Pretty Face"
memes.description: The opium-den woman's whispered revelation about Dorian Gray — a piece of underworld gossip that contains the novel's supernatural truth in crude, folkloric form.

memes.label: "Like a Common Gardener Walking with a Rose"
memes.description: The narrator's striking simile describing James Vane walking beside his sister Sibyl — a single image that captures the class tension, protective love, and doomed beauty at the heart of the Vane family tragedy.

memes.label: Hetty Merton
memes.description: A simple village girl whom Dorian Gray claims to have spared from seduction in a supposed act of reformation — an act that proves to be rooted in vanity rather than genuine moral change.

memes.label: London High Society
memes.description: The aristocratic social world of late-Victorian London that Dorian Gray moves through — simultaneously a stage for his beauty, a mechanism of moral judgment, and the force field of "the terror of society" that governs the novel's characters.

memes.label: "Such hideous things were for the darkness, not for the day"
memes.description: "The morning after murdering Basil Hallward, Dorian Gray wakes to find the dead body still sitting upstairs in the sunlight — and recoils at the intrusion of horror into the daylight world."

memes.label: Horror
memes.description: The visceral response to moral and physical decay in Dorian Gray — the emotion that registers the truth the intellect denies. (008-016)

memes.label: Humanity Takes Itself Too Seriously
memes.description: Lord Henry's diagnosis of the human condition — that seriousness is the original sin, and that laughter is the lost art of the caveman — offered during the lunch party at Lady Agatha's in Chapter 3.
aliases: humanity takes itself too seriously it is the worlds original sin

memes.label: The husbands of very beautiful women belong to the criminal classes
memes.description: "The husbands of very beautiful women belong to the criminal classes."

relations.label: A man can be happy with any woman

memes.label: Hyde Park
memes.description: London's great fashionable park — the daily stage for aristocratic display, where Society rides, walks, and performs its rituals of visibility, and where the novel maps the boundaries between those who belong and those who do not.

memes.label: Hypocrisy
memes.description: The gap between public virtue and private vice — a central theme in a novel where the protagonist preserves an unblemished appearance while his hidden portrait records his moral decay. (017-032)

memes.label: I Can Believe Anything
memes.description: Lord Henry's epigrammatic redefinition of belief — not as credulity but as a taste for the impossible — delivered moments before Basil reveals the secret of his portrait.
aliases: provided that it is quite incredible

memes.label: I Can Sympathize with Everything Except Suffering
memes.description: "Lord Henry refuses sympathy for suffering on aesthetic grounds: pain is ugly, and one should sympathize only with beauty."

memes.label: I Choose My Friends for Their Good Looks
memes.description: "Lord Henry's taxonomy of human relations: friends for looks, acquaintances for character, enemies for intellect."

memes.label: "I Feel Proud, Terribly Proud"
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's paradoxical confession — though she feels beneath Prince Charming, love elevates rather than humiliates her, transforming awe into pride.

memes.label: "I have grown sick of shadows"
memes.description: "Sibyl Vane declares that her love for Dorian Gray has shattered the illusion of theatrical art — real life has made stage shadows unbearable."

memes.label: I Have Never Searched for Happiness
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's confession to the Duchess of Monmouth: he has never sought happiness, only pleasure — a creed and a tragedy in one sentence."

memes.label: I have no terror of death
memes.description: "I have no terror of death. It is the coming of death that terrifies me."

relations.label: only horrible thing in the world is ennui

memes.label: I have not been in love for a whole week
memes.description: "\"I have not been in love for a whole week — not, in fact, since Madame de Ferrol left town.\""

memes.label: I have put too much of myself into it
memes.description: Basil Hallward refuses to exhibit the portrait of Dorian Gray, confessing that he has invested too much of his own soul in the painting — a statement that establishes the central motif of art as self-revelation and anticipates the supernatural bond between artist, subject, and image.

memes.label: I Like Men Who Have a Future and Women Who Have a Past
memes.description: "Lord Henry's dinner-party epigram: a symmetrical provocation on gender, time, and social scandal."
aliases: men with future women with past

memes.label: More Real Than Life
memes.description: Lord Henry's paradoxical declaration that performance is more authentic than lived experience — "I love acting. It is so much more real than life."

memes.label: "I love him because he is like what love himself should be"
memes.description: "Sibyl Vane's explanation of why she loves Dorian Gray — not for who he is, but for what he embodies."

relations.label: To see him is to worship him; to know him is to trust him

memes.label: I love scandals about other people
memes.description: "I love scandals about other people, but scandals about myself don't interest me. They have not got the charm of novelty."

memes.label: I Said It Fascinated Me
memes.description: "Dorian corrects Lord Henry: 'I didn't say I liked it, Harry. I said it fascinated me. There is a great difference.'"

memes.label: "I shall show you my soul"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's defiant declaration that he will show Basil Hallward the portrait — the soul that 'only God can see' — sealing Basil's fate."

relations.label: Sin is a thing that writes itself

memes.label: I Should Like to Be Somebody Else
memes.description: "I hope it is not about myself. I am tired of myself to-night. I should like to be somebody else."

memes.label: "I shudder at the thought of being free"
memes.description: "Sibyl Vane's paradoxical embrace of love's bondage — a declaration that anticipates the novel's themes of freedom and fate."

relations.label: To see him is to worship him; to know him is to trust him
Each man lived his own life and paid his own price for living it

memes.label: "I want the Dorian Gray I used to paint"
memes.description: "Basil Hallward mourns the loss of the innocent young man he once painted, sensing that Dorian has changed irreparably."

memes.label: "If a man treats life artistically, his brain is his heart"
memes.description: "Lord Henry, reflecting on Basil Hallward's artistic decline, delivers an aphorism that distills the aestheticist doctrine: when life is treated as art, intellect and emotion merge."

memes.label: If he ever does you any wrong, I shall kill him
memes.description: "As sure as there is a God in heaven, if he ever does you any wrong, I shall kill him."

memes.label: "If He Ever Does You Any Wrong, I Shall Kill Him"
memes.description: James Vane's ominous vow to protect his sister Sibyl — a promise spoken in love that will echo through the novel as prophecy, driving the revenge plot of the final chapters.

memes.label: If One Doesn't Talk About a Thing
memes.description: Dorian Gray, echoing Lord Henry's philosophy, declares that silence erases reality — a justification for evading moral confrontation after Sibyl Vane's death.

memes.label: Illusion
memes.description: The veil of false perception that sustains characters throughout the novel — illusion in The Picture of Dorian Gray operates as both a psychological defense against unpleasant truths and a core theme in Wilde's exploration of the relationship between appearance, reality, and self-knowledge.

memes.label: "The Imagination Set Remorse to Dog the Feet of Sin"
memes.description: "The narrator reflects that it is imagination — not external justice — that makes guilt inescapable: \"It was the imagination that set remorse to dog the feet of sin\" — the novel's most concentrated statement on the psychology of conscience."

memes.label: In the Opium Den
memes.description: Dorian Gray visits a squalid opium den near the London docks, seeking oblivion from the guilt of Basil Hallward's murder, leading to a near-fatal confrontation with James Vane.

memes.label: Individualism
memes.description: A philosophical ideal championed by Lord Henry — the doctrine that personal development is the highest aim of life, positioned against conventional morality. (006-040)

memes.label: Individuality
memes.description: A quality Lord Henry Wotton prizes as the mark of an interesting personality, contrasting it with the colourlessness he attributes to unselfish people (006-019).

memes.label: Influence
memes.description: The power one person exerts over another — in the novel, primarily Lord Henry Wotton's corrosive influence over Dorian Gray, which drives the central tragedy.

memes.label: Innocence
memes.description: The state of moral purity that the novel's characters lose or corrupt — a lost paradise that Dorian Gray, in his final chapter, briefly longs to recover. (011-003)

memes.label: Intellect
memes.description: The faculty of reason and abstract thought, which Lord Henry Wotton elevates as a mark of distinction and a weapon against convention — and which the novel ultimately reveals as insufficient to govern life or to resist the claims of beauty and passion. (001-045)

memes.label: "It had been like conscience to him"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray acknowledges the portrait has served as his conscience, and resolves to destroy it — the final moral reckoning before his death."

memes.label: It Has Destroyed Me
memes.description: Dorian Gray's confession to Basil Hallward in the attic schoolroom — "I was wrong. It has destroyed me" — upon revealing the corrupted portrait.

memes.label: It Is Never Too Late Dorian
memes.description: Basil Hallward's desperate plea to Dorian to repent — the novel's most direct call to moral reclamation — delivered in the fateful confrontation of Chapter 13.
aliases: it is never too late Dorian
let us kneel down and try

memes.label: It Is Only Shallow People
memes.description: "Dorian Gray defends his emotional detachment after Sibyl Vane's death: 'It is only shallow people who require years to get rid of an emotion.'"

memes.label: "It is only shallow people who require years to get rid of an emotion"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's declaration of emotional self-mastery — a sentence in which he speaks with Lord Henry's voice, revealing how thoroughly the philosophy of aesthetic detachment has colonised his thinking."

relations.label: There is always something ridiculous about the emotions of people whom one has ceased to love.
There are only two kinds of people who are really fascinating.

memes.label: It Is Only the Sacred Things That Are Worth Touching
memes.description: Lord Henry's reply when Dorian protests that Sibyl Vane is "sacred" — "It is only the sacred things that are worth touching, Dorian" — a paradox that transforms reverence into an invitation to violation.
aliases: only the sacred things are worth touching

memes.label: It Is the Face of My Soul
memes.description: Dorian Gray's devastating confession to Basil Hallward — five words that collapse the distance between the beautiful man and the hideous portrait.

memes.label: It is the feet of clay that make the gold of the image precious
memes.description: 'Lord Henry articulates his aesthetic paradox: imperfection — the "feet of clay" — is what makes beauty truly valuable, as he critiques the Duchess of Monmouth for being too clever to possess the charm of weakness.'

memes.label: It Is the Spectator, and Not Life, That Art Really Mirrors
memes.description: "Oscar Wilde's Preface aphorism: art reflects the perceiving consciousness, not objective reality."

memes.label: It Was a Poisonous Book
memes.description: "Wilde's narrator describes the 'yellow book' Lord Henry lends to Dorian: 'It was a poisonous book. The heavy odour of incense seemed to cling about its pages and to trouble the brain.'"

memes.label: "It was his beauty that had ruined him"
memes.description: "The novel's closing verdict on Dorian Gray — his beauty and the youth he prayed for became the instruments of his destruction."

memes.label: Such Love as Michelangelo Had Known
memes.description: The narrator reflects on the nature of Basil Hallward's devotion to Dorian — "It was such love as Michelangelo had known, and Montaigne, and Winckelmann, and Shakespeare himself."

memes.label: "It would kill the past, and when that was dead, he would be free"
memes.description: "In the novel's climactic scene, Dorian Gray seizes the knife that killed Basil Hallward and stabs the portrait — believing that destroying the evidence of his sins will set him free from the past."

relations.label: It had been like conscience to him
Each man lived his own life and paid his own price for living it

memes.label: James Vane
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's protective brother — a sailor who vows revenge on "Prince Charming" for his sister's death and pursues Dorian Gray for eighteen years before being killed in a hunting accident.

memes.label: Jewels
memes.description: Precious stones and ornamentation that represent Dorian Gray's obsession with material beauty as a substitute for moral substance.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: A concept invoked ironically throughout The Picture of Dorian Gray, where characters appeal to justice as a rhetorical device while the novel's moral universe offers no true justice for its central crimes (011-009).

memes.label: Kept Himself Unspotted from the World
memes.description: A phrase used twice by the narrator — first sincerely in Chapter 2, then with savage irony in Chapter 11 — to describe Dorian Gray's deceptively pure appearance.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: In Dorian Gray, knowledge is a double-edged gift — sought as a form of power and experience, yet often linked to loss, corruption, and the burden of awareness. (001-012)

memes.label: Knowledge Would Be Fatal
memes.description: Lord Henry tells the Duchess that knowing whether one is in love would destroy the charm — "Knowledge would be fatal. It is the uncertainty that charms one."

memes.label: Lady Agatha
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's aunt and a London society hostess whose dinner parties provide the setting for key conversations in the novel's opening chapters. (001-071)

memes.label: Lady Brandon
memes.description: A London society hostess whose crush party inadvertently introduces Dorian Gray to Lord Henry Wotton, setting the novel's central relationship in motion. (001-034)

memes.label: Lady Gwendolen
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's sister, a society beauty whom Dorian Gray meets at the opera; her reputation is later destroyed through her association with him. (009-003) (012-023)

memes.label: Lady Hamilton
memes.description: A historical beauty referenced in Chapter 11 in connection with one of Dorian Gray's ancestral portraits; the comparison evokes the celebrated Emma, Lady Hamilton. (011-032)

memes.label: Lady Narborough
memes.description: A clever, sharp-tongued society hostess whose dinner party in Chapter 15 provides the setting for some of the novel's most incisive social satire and the first signs of Dorian Gray's social unraveling.

memes.label: Lady Radley
memes.description: The wife of Lord Radley, Dorian Gray's guardian, who gives Dorian a mother-of-pearl gift — one of several society references (006-025, 010-037).

memes.label: Lady Ruxton
memes.description: An overdressed society woman who attends Lady Narborough's dinner party in Chapter 15, where her exchange with Lord Henry provides one of the chapter's sharpest moments of social satire.

memes.label: Laughter Is Not at All a Bad Beginning for a Friendship
memes.description: "Lord Henry's aphorism on laughter as the bookend of friendship: a good beginning and the best possible ending."

memes.label: "Life has been your art"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton declares that Dorian Gray has achieved the ultimate aesthetic ideal: living life itself as a work of art."

memes.label: Life has everything in store for you
memes.description: "\"Life has everything in store for you, Dorian. There is nothing that you, with your extraordinary good looks, will not be able to do.\""

memes.label: "Life has everything in store for you, Dorian"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton assures Dorian Gray that his beauty will open every door, a seductive promise that steers Dorian away from grief and toward a life of aesthetic self-cultivation."

memes.label: Life Is a Great Disappointment
memes.description: "At Lady Narborough's dinner party, the evening after murdering Basil Hallward, Dorian Gray sighs: 'Life is a great disappointment.'"

memes.label: "Life is not governed by will or intention"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton declares that life is governed not by conscious choice but by nerves, sensations, and unconscious forces — a philosophy that absolves Dorian of moral responsibility."

relations.label: The things one feels absolutely certain about are never true
He used to wonder at the shallow psychology of those who conceive the ego as a thing simple.
The terror of society, which is the basis of morals...

memes.label: Life Itself Was the First of the Arts
memes.description: "The narrator's summary of Dorian Gray's aesthetic creed: life itself is the first and greatest art — all other arts are merely preparation for it."

memes.label: He Loathed His Own Beauty
memes.description: The climactic moment of the novel — Dorian Gray "loathed his own beauty, and flinging the mirror on the floor, crushed it into silver splinters beneath his heel" — before stabbing the portrait and dying.

memes.label: Lord Fermor
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's genial, rough-mannered uncle, who provides the genealogical intelligence about Dorian Gray's parentage — the key to understanding Dorian's social position and the scandal of his birth.

memes.label: Lord Henry Wotton
memes.description: Aristocratic wit and philosopher of hedonism whose influence drives the novel's central tragedy.

memes.label: Lord Henry's Philosophy
memes.description: The aesthetic and philosophical worldview expounded by Lord Henry Wotton throughout the novel, centred on the pursuit of beauty, pleasure, and sensation as the highest aims of life. (002-048)

memes.label: Lord Kelso
memes.description: Dorian Gray's maternal grandfather, a cruel aristocrat who disowned his daughter Margaret Devereux after she married beneath her station. (003-009)

memes.label: "I love acting. It is so much more real than life"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton declares that theatrical performance surpasses lived experience in authenticity, inverting the conventional hierarchy of art and life."

memes.label: Love Is a More Wonderful Thing than Art
memes.description: "Basil Hallward to Dorian: Don't talk like that about any one you love, Dorian. Love is a more wonderful thing than Art."
aliases: don't talk like that about anyone you love

memes.label: "Love Is More than Money"
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's declaration of love's supremacy over material concerns — a statement of pure romantic idealism spoken in Chapter 5, whose tragic irony the novel will fully expose.

memes.label: "Love Makes People Good"
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's gentle rebuke to her brother James — love transforms character, and what you said was wicked because it came from jealousy, not love.

memes.label: Mad Hungers That Grew More Ravenous
memes.description: "The narrator's diagnosis of Dorian's accelerating corruption: his curiosity, once stirred, became insatiable — mad hungers that grew more ravenous as he fed them."

memes.label: A man can be happy with any woman
memes.description: "\"A man can be happy with any woman, as long as he does not love her.\""

memes.label: Marble Arch
memes.description: The monumental white marble landmark at the northeast corner of Hyde Park — a transit point for Sibyl and James Vane's last afternoon together in Chapter 5, and the site of Lord Henry Wotton's street-preacher anecdote in Chapter 19, marking the boundary between the novel's aristocratic West End and the working-class world beyond.

memes.label: Margaret Devereux
memes.description: Dorian Gray's mother, an extraordinarily beautiful woman who eloped with a penniless man and died within a year of her son's birth, disowned by her father Lord Kelso. (003-009)

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: A social institution that the novel treats through Lord Henry Wotton's cynical aphorisms, framing marriage as a constraint on individuality and a source of mutual deception (001-017).

memes.label: A Matter of Life and Death
memes.description: "The exchange between Alan Campbell and Dorian Gray — 'But you said it was a matter of life and death' / 'Yes: it is a matter of life and death, Alan, and to more than one person' — the leverage that forces Campbell into complicity."
aliases: it is a matter of life and death

memes.label: Mayfair
memes.description: The fashionable London district where Dorian Gray maintains his residence — the public face of his life, contrasting with the hidden corruption of his private quarters.

memes.label: "the remembrance even of joy having its bitterness"
memes.description: "The narrator reflects on the bittersweet nature of memory — how even joyful recollections carry pain, in a Gothic-inflected meditation on waking before dawn."

memes.label: Memory and Forgetting
memes.description: A thematic pair in The Picture of Dorian Gray tracing the tension between memory as moral record and forgetting as the precondition for Dorian's life of unaccountable pleasure.

memes.label: Memory, Like a Horrible Malady
memes.description: In the opium den, memory becomes a disease for Dorian Gray — "Memory, like a horrible malady, was eating his soul away."

memes.label: Men Marry Because They Are Tired
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's epigrammatic advice to Dorian Gray — "Never marry at all" — with his cynical diagnosis of why men and women enter into marriage.

memes.label: The Mind of the Thoroughly Well-Informed Man Is a Dreadful Thing
memes.description: "Lord Henry's devastating image of the educated mind as a junk shop: 'a bric-à-brac shop, all monsters and dust, with everything priced above its proper value.'"

memes.label: Moderation Is a Fatal Thing
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram at Lady Narborough's dinner party: moderation is fatal, excess is the true feast."
aliases: enough is as bad as a meal

relations.label: the only way to get rid of a temptation is to yield to it

memes.label: The Moments of a Life That Is Itself But a Moment
memes.description: "The practical conclusion of Dorian Gray's new Hedonism: concentrate on the moments of a life that is itself but a moment — experience as its own justification."

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: The system of ethical judgment that the novel simultaneously rejects (in its Preface) and enforces (in its plot) — the unresolved tension at the heart of Wilde's aesthetic project.

memes.label: Mortality
memes.description: The consciousness of death and the fleeting nature of life, which in The Picture of Dorian Gray drives both the desire to transcend mortal limits and the horror that follows (001-038).

memes.label: Mr. Chapman
memes.description: A gentleman who attends Lady Narborough's dinner party in Chapter 15, interacting with Dorian Gray (015-005, 015-053, 015-072).

memes.label: Mr. Erskine
memes.description: An elderly gentleman of Treadley who appears at Lady Agatha's dinner party in Chapter 3, serving as a foil to Lord Henry's youthful wit with his gentle, old-fashioned manner. (003-036)

memes.label: Mr. Hubbard
memes.description: The celebrated frame-maker of South Audley Street who moves Dorian Gray's portrait to the attic—a seemingly mundane service that enables the concealment of the portrait's transformation.

memes.label: Mr. Isaacs
memes.description: The Jewish theatre manager who employs Sibyl Vane and holds the Vane family in financial bondage through loans and patronage. (005-002)

memes.label: Mrs. Leaf
memes.description: Dorian Gray's elderly housekeeper, whose bustling presence in Chapter 10 and silent grief in Chapter 20 frame the novel's narrative of concealment and exposure.

memes.label: Mrs. Vandeleur
memes.description: One of Lady Agatha's oldest friends at a Chapter 3 luncheon, embodying conventional Victorian morality in contrast to Lord Henry's philosophy (003-036, 003-068).

memes.label: Mrs. Vane
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's mother, a faded, debt-ridden actress who lives vicariously through her daughter's stage career and romantic prospects. (005-001)

memes.label: Multiply Our Personalities
memes.description: "Is insincerity such a terrible thing? It is merely a method by which we can multiply our personalities."

relations.label: I love scandals about other people

memes.label: Murder
memes.description: "The novel's central act of violence: Dorian Gray's killing of Basil Hallward in Chapter 13, and the recurring motif of murder that haunts the narrative from James Vane's threats to Dorian's final confession. (013-038)"

memes.label: The Murder of Basil Hallward
memes.description: The climactic crime of the novel — Dorian Gray's murder of the artist Basil Hallward in Chapter 13, the decisive sin that makes his damnation irreversible and the portrait's most damning testimony.

memes.label: "My God! And I Would Have Murdered You!"
memes.description: James Vane's cry of horror when he sees Dorian Gray's youthful face under the lamp — the avenger discovers he has nearly killed an innocent man, or so he believes.

memes.label: Native Land of the Hypocrite
memes.description: "Confronted by Basil about the rumours surrounding him, Dorian deflects with a critique of English society: 'My dear fellow, you forget that we are in the native land of the hypocrite.'"

memes.label: The True Nature of the Senses
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's revisionist history of the senses: they have never been truly understood — civilization has tried to starve them into submission rather than making them elements of a new spirituality."

memes.label: "Never Marry at All"
memes.description: "Lord Henry's aphorism on marriage — \"Never marry at all, Dorian. Men marry because they are tired; women, because they are curious: both are disappointed\" — delivered at Lady Agatha's luncheon."
aliases: men marry because they are tired

memes.label: Never Put One's Worship into Words
memes.description: Basil Hallward, after confessing his intense admiration for Dorian, reflects that articulating devotion diminishes it — "Perhaps one should never put one's worship into words."
aliases: one should never put one's worship into words

memes.label: "You never say a moral thing, and you never do a wrong thing"
memes.description: "Basil Hallward observes the paradox of Lord Henry's character: he talks like a cynic but lives like a gentleman, never practicing the immoralities he preaches."

memes.label: No Civilized Man Ever Regrets a Pleasure
memes.description: Lord Henry declares that the capacity for pleasure without regret is the mark of civilization — "No civilized man ever regrets a pleasure, and no uncivilized man ever knows what a pleasure is."

memes.label: No Life Is Spoiled But One Whose Growth Is Arrested
memes.description: "Lord Henry's doctrine of growth: the only truly spoiled life is one whose development has been halted — a philosophy that justifies every experiment."

memes.label: Nothing Can Cure the Soul but the Senses
memes.description: Lord Henry's aphorism — "Nothing can cure the soul but the senses, just as nothing can cure the senses but the soul" — whispered to Dorian as he buries his face in lilac blossoms in Basil Hallward's garden.
aliases: cure the soul by means of the senses

memes.label: Nothing Is Serious Nowadays
memes.description: "Nothing is serious nowadays. At least nothing should be."

relations.label: I love scandals about other people

memes.label: Nowadays Most People Die of Creeping Common Sense
memes.description: Lord Henry's paradox of prudence — that common sense, far from being a safeguard, is a slow death, and that mistakes are the only things one never regrets — delivered at Lady Agatha's lunch in Chapter 3.
aliases: most people die of a sort of creeping common sense
the only things one never regrets are ones mistakes

memes.label: The Price of Everything and the Value of Nothing
memes.description: Lord Henry's famous epigram on the philistinism of modern society — one of Wilde's most quoted lines.

memes.label: "Nowadays People Know the Price of Everything and the Value of Nothing"
memes.description: "Lord Henry's critique of modern materialism — \"Nowadays people know the price of everything and the value of nothing\" — one of the novel's most quoted epigrams, distinguishing economic cost from true worth."

memes.label: The One Charm of Marriage
memes.description: "Lord Henry's cynical aphorism: the one charm of marriage is that it makes a life of deception absolutely necessary for both parties."

memes.label: You have been the one person in my life who has really influenced my art
memes.description: "You have been the one person in my life who has really influenced my art. Whatever I have done that is good, I owe to you."

relations.label: one should never put one's worship into words

memes.label: One Should Never Do Anything One Cannot Talk About After Dinner
memes.description: "Lord Henry: 'One should never do anything that one cannot talk about after dinner' — an aphorism on murder delivered to the murderer himself over coffee."
aliases: never do anything you cannot talk about after dinner

memes.label: "There are only two kinds of people who are really fascinating"
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram dividing humanity into those who know everything and those who know nothing — a characteristically paradoxical remark that flattens the complexity of human experience into a witty binary."

memes.label: Opium
memes.description: The narcotic substance that symbolizes Dorian Gray's desperate attempt to escape guilt and memory through sensory oblivion.

memes.label: Opium Dens Where One Could Buy Oblivion
memes.description: "Narrator: 'There were opium dens where one could buy oblivion, dens of horror where the memory of old sins could be destroyed by the madness of sins that were new' — the novel's introduction to the East End underworld."

memes.label: Oscar Wilde
memes.description: Oscar Wilde (1854–1900), Irish poet and playwright, author of The Picture of Dorian Gray (1890/1891), whose aesthetic doctrines and epigrammatic style pervade the novel.

memes.label: "Our Proverbs Want Rewriting"
memes.description: Sibyl Vane's declaration that love rewrites all received wisdom — her most characteristic expression of romantic idealism, spoken to her brother Jim on the eve of her theatrical triumph.

relations.label: love flies in through the window

memes.label: Paradox
memes.description: A self-contradictory statement that, upon examination, reveals a deeper truth; Lord Henry Wotton's primary mode of speech and the rhetorical engine of Wilde's social critique (002-028).

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: A central thematic force in The Picture of Dorian Gray, contrasted with caprice and mediated through Lord Henry's philosophy of controlled experience.

memes.label: The Passion for Impossible Things
memes.description: The narrator describes how Lord Henry's theories stirred in Dorian "the passion for impossible things" — a phrase that captures the novel's diagnosis of dangerous desire.

memes.label: "Passion Makes One Think in a Circle"
memes.description: The narrator's psychological observation as Dorian Gray races toward the opium den — passion traps the mind in repetition, and Dorian's circular thoughts reveal a soul devouring itself.

memes.label: Perfumes
memes.description: Fragrances and the art of perfumery that Wilde uses to represent the aesthetic cultivation of sensory experience as both refinement and escape.

memes.label: Perhaps one never seems so much at one's ease as when one has to play a part
memes.description: The narrator observes that Dorian Gray's composure at Lady Narborough's dinner party — mere hours after murdering Basil Hallward — reveals how performance becomes second nature to those leading a double life.

memes.label: "Perhaps one should never put one's worship into words"
memes.description: "Basil Hallward reflects that the act of articulating devotion diminishes it — a principle that governs his relationship to art and to Dorian Gray."

memes.label: I Like Persons Better Than Principles
memes.description: "Lord Henry's declaration of personal allegiance over abstract morality: persons with no principles are the best of all."

memes.label: Philip Herbert
memes.description: An ancestor of Dorian Gray whose portrait hangs in the picture-gallery at Selby Royal, described by Francis Osborne in his Memoires on the Reigns of Queen Elizabeth and King James. (011-032)

memes.label: Piccadilly
memes.description: The grand London thoroughfare running from Piccadilly Circus to Hyde Park Corner — where Dorian Gray strolls during his pre-Sibyl wanderings, embodying the fashionable West End world of spectacle and class observation.

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: The guiding principle of Lord Henry's philosophy and the object of Dorian Gray's lifelong pursuit — pleasure as an aesthetic and ethical ideal that, pursued to excess, becomes a form of self-destruction.

memes.label: "Pleasure Is the Only Thing Worth Having a Theory About"
memes.description: "Lord Henry's declaration that pleasure is Nature's test of approval — \"Pleasure is the only thing worth having a theory about\" — the philosophical center of his hedonistic creed."

memes.label: Poisoned by a Book
memes.description: "After cataloguing historical poisons, Wilde's narrator delivers the devastating verdict: 'Dorian Gray had been poisoned by a book.'"

relations.label: the earlier description of the book as poisonous
realize his conception of the beautiful

memes.label: "There Is No Such Thing as a Moral or an Immoral Book"
memes.description: The Preface's most famous aphorism — Wilde's declaration of art's moral autonomy, which the novel simultaneously asserts and complicates through its Gothic morality tale.

memes.label: Prince Charming Rules Life for Us
memes.description: "Sibyl Vane declares her love for the mysterious young man she calls Prince Charming: 'We don't want him any more, Mother. Prince Charming rules life for us now.'"

memes.label: Psychology
memes.description: The novel's implicit theory of mind — Wilde's exploration of human motivation, the structure of the self, and the limits of self-knowledge, articulated through narrative commentary and Lord Henry's epigrams. (004-104)

memes.label: Punctuality Is the Thief of Time
memes.description: The narrator's explanation of Lord Henry's habitual lateness — his principle that "punctuality is the thief of time" — as Dorian waits for him in the Mayfair library.

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: "The consequence of sin, both external and internal — from the portrait's visible degradation to Dorian's final, desperate prayer: \"Smite us for our iniquities.\" (020-004)"

memes.label: Remorse
memes.description: The painful awareness of guilt and regret for wrongdoing, which the novel explores both as a potential path to redemption and as a torment to be escaped through distraction (006-045).

memes.label: Repentance
memes.description: The act of feeling remorse and seeking forgiveness, which Basil Hallward urges upon Dorian Gray as a path to redemption (013-028).

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: The social esteem that Victorian society values above all — the surface Dorian preserves while his private life decays, and the currency Lord Henry cynically manipulates.

memes.label: Romance Lives by Repetition
memes.description: "Romance lives by repetition, and repetition converts an appetite into an art."
aliases: repetition converts an appetite into an art

memes.label: "Rose-white boyhood"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's anguished longing for the lost innocence of his youth, echoing Lord Henry's earlier phrase."

relations.label: It would kill the past, and when that was dead, he would be free

memes.label: Scandal
memes.description: The currency of high society in Wilde's novel — a force that both fascinates and destroys, driving the plot through whispered rumours and public disgrace. (018-034)

memes.label: "Scepticism is the beginning of faith"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton redefines scepticism not as the enemy of faith but as its necessary precondition."

memes.label: See Your Soul
memes.description: Basil Hallward's anguished declaration — "Before I could answer that, I should have to see your soul" — the line that triggers Dorian's fateful decision to reveal the portrait.
aliases: I should have to see your soul

memes.label: Selby Royal
memes.description: Dorian Gray's country estate where James Vane is killed in a hunting accident — a pastoral setting that becomes the site of the novel's last external threat to Dorian.

memes.label: Shallow Sorrows and Shallow Loves Live On
memes.description: Wilde's narrator observes that superficial emotions endure while profound ones consume themselves — a psychological law that explains why Dorian Gray survives his crimes.
aliases: the loves and sorrows that are great are destroyed by their own plenitude

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: The painful emotion of humiliation and disgrace, which the novel explores through Dorian Gray's hidden portrait — the visible "mask of his shame" that bears the moral consequences of his life (008-015).

memes.label: She is all the great heroines of the world in one
memes.description: Dorian Gray's ecstatic description of Sibyl Vane reveals that he loves her not as an individual but as the embodiment of all dramatic heroines — an idealization that makes the collapse of his love inevitable.

memes.label: She Is Better Than Good
memes.description: Lord Henry's epigrammatic dismissal of moral categories in favor of beauty — "she is better than good—she is beautiful" — a compressed statement of his aesthetic philosophy.

memes.label: "She knew nothing, but she had everything that he had lost"
memes.description: "The narrative voice contrasts Hetty Merton's ignorance with Dorian Gray's loss — a single sentence that encapsulates the novel's meditation on innocence and experience."

memes.label: "She will never come to life again now"
memes.description: "Dorian Gray murmurs his realization that Sibyl Vane is truly dead, a line that marks the collapse of his romantic fantasy of her as an endlessly renewable artistic figure."

memes.label: "Sibyl Has a Mother; I Had None"
memes.description: Mrs Vane's defensive murmur — a moment of self-revelation that explains the emotional economy of the Vane household and the generational pattern of abandonment.

memes.label: Sibyl Vane
memes.description: The young Shakespearean actress with whom Dorian Gray falls in love — and whose rejection and subsequent suicide mark the first visible change in the portrait and Dorian's first moral transgression.

memes.label: Suicide of Sibyl Vane
memes.description: The suicide of actress Sibyl Vane after Dorian Gray cruelly rejects her, triggering the portrait's first supernatural change.

memes.label: Sibyl's Bad Performance
memes.description: The evening at the theatre when Sibyl Vane gives a lifeless performance, leading Dorian Gray to publicly reject her and setting off the chain of events culminating in her suicide.

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The moral transgressions that accumulate on Dorian Gray's soul and register visibly on his portrait — the hidden record of a life lived without ethical constraint, and the novel's central structural principle.

memes.label: Sin Is a Thing That Writes Itself
memes.description: Basil Hallward's warning to Dorian Gray — "Sin is a thing that writes itself across a man's face. It cannot be concealed" — a statement whose literal falsity in Dorian's case precipitates Basil's death.
aliases: sin writes itself across a man's face

memes.label: "Sin is a thing that writes itself across a man's face"
memes.description: "Basil Hallward's assertion that moral corruption is physically legible — a statement of faith in the visibility of virtue that the entire plot of the novel proceeds to demolish."

memes.label: Sin Is the Only Real Colour-Element Left
memes.description: Lord Henry's aesthetic complaint against Victorian sobriety — that the drab costume of the nineteenth century has drained life of colour, leaving sin as the sole remaining vividness — spoken at tea in Basil Hallward's studio.
aliases: sin is the only real colour element left in modern life

memes.label: "I represent to you all the sins you have never had the courage to commit"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton explains his fascination for Dorian Gray: he embodies the prohibited, making vicarious sin seductive and safe."

memes.label: Sir Henry Ashton
memes.description: A former friend of Dorian Gray forced to leave England under scandal, cited by Lord Henry as evidence of ruined lives surrounding Dorian (012-019, 012-020, 020-016).

memes.label: Sir Thomas Burdon
memes.description: A Radical Member of Parliament and guest at Lady Agatha's dinner party in Chapter 3, whose earnestness and conventional opinions serve as a foil to Lord Henry's subversive wit. (003-036)

memes.label: Smite Us for Our Iniquities
memes.description: Dorian Gray's final theological insight — that punishment is preferable to impunity, and that true justice would strike rather than forgive.
aliases: not forgive us our sins but smite us

memes.label: Only God Who Sees the Soul
memes.description: "Dorian to Basil before revealing the portrait: So you think that it is only God who sees the soul, Basil? Draw that curtain back, and you will see mine."

memes.label: Sold himself to the devil for a pretty face
memes.description: "They say he has sold himself to the devil for a pretty face. It's nigh on eighteen years since I met him. He hasn't changed much since then."

memes.label: Something Fatal About a Portrait
memes.description: Dorian Gray's refusal to sit for Basil again — "There is something fatal about a portrait. It has a life of its own." — spoken after he has seen the painted image begin to change.
aliases: a portrait has a life of its own

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: The spiritual essence that Dorian Gray barters away for eternal youth — the invisible core of moral identity that the portrait makes visible, and whose corruption constitutes the novel's central horror.

memes.label: The Soul Is a Terrible Reality
memes.description: Dorian Gray's anguished reply to Lord Henry — "The soul is a terrible reality. It can be bought, and sold, and bartered away" — the novel's most explicit statement of its supernatural premise.

memes.label: South Audley Street
memes.description: A Mayfair street intersecting Grosvenor Square — home to a celebrated frame-maker and the site of Basil Hallward's final, unrecognized passage past Dorian Gray in the fog of Chapter 12.

memes.label: Sphinxes Without Secrets
memes.description: "Sphinxes without secrets."

relations.label: Courage has passed from men to women

memes.label: The Spiritualizing of the Senses
memes.description: "Dorian Gray's ambition: to elaborate a new scheme of life that finds its highest realization in the spiritualizing of the senses — transforming bodily experience into a form of spirituality."

memes.label: "It represents the survival of the pushing"
memes.description: "Lord Henry Wotton satirises Darwinian evolution by reducing 'survival of the fittest' to social pushiness."

relations.label: only horrible thing in the world is ennui

memes.label: Temptation
memes.description: A pivotal moral and psychological force in The Picture of Dorian Gray — temptation operates as both the mechanism of Lord Henry's influence and the test of character that Dorian repeatedly fails, culminating in the novel's central ethical drama.

memes.label: "The Only Way to Get Rid of a Temptation Is to Yield to It"
memes.description: Lord Henry Wotton's most seductive and dangerous epigram — the philosophical license for Dorian Gray's descent into hedonism, encapsulating the novel's critique of aestheticism as ethics.

memes.label: The Terrible Pleasure of a Double Life
memes.description: After murdering Basil Hallward and destroying the evidence, Dorian Gray attends a dinner party and feels "keenly the terrible pleasure of a double life" — the novel's sharpest summary of his divided existence.
aliases: terrible pleasure of a double life

memes.label: That Awful Thing, a Woman's Memory
memes.description: Lord Henry's epigram on the tenacity of female recollection — "That awful thing, a woman's memory!"

memes.label: The Royal Academy of Arts
memes.description: The Royal Academy of Arts, a prestigious London art institution that Basil Hallward deliberately avoids, preferring to exhibit at the Grosvenor Gallery instead.

memes.label: The Aim of Life Is Self-Development
memes.description: Lord Henry's manifesto of individualism — that self-realization is the highest human purpose — delivered to Dorian Gray in the garden of Basil Hallward's studio in Chapter 2.
aliases: the aim of life is self-development

memes.label: The Albany
memes.description: The exclusive bachelor chambers on Piccadilly where Lord Fermor lives — the setting for Lord Henry Wotton's visit in Chapter 3, where he extracts the secret of Dorian Gray's parentage while absorbing his uncle's reactionary social philosophy.

memes.label: The Basis of Every Scandal Is an Immoral Certainty
memes.description: "Lord Henry's epigram: scandal requires not just misbehavior but the certainty of it — uncertainty is what makes things charming."

memes.label: The Basis of Optimism Is Sheer Terror
memes.description: Lord Henry argues that the impulse to think well of others is rooted in self-protective fear — "The basis of optimism is sheer terror."
aliases: reason we think well of others
